--- Where to Look and What to Look For ---

-: Hey, guys, welcome back.
Once you've prepared to look for product management jobs,
and you've got that good online brand,
and you think you can point to some relevant experience,
you have to know where to go to look for the jobs
and really what you want to be looking for.
In this lecture, we're gonna talk about three things.
One, looking at your own company,
two, networking, and three, looking online.
Firstly, it's important to understand
that if you're really serious
about getting into product management,
then the best place, by far, that you can look for a job
is in your own company.
Trust me on that.
It's so much easier to move internally
than it is to get a new job as a product manager
with no previous experience.
If you already have a product management team
at your office, your company,
ask to shadow some product managers in meetings
to learn more about what they're working on.
What you should do here is demonstrate
that you're interested in helping them out,
and offer to do some work in your spare time for their team.
The goal here is to learn as much as you can.
If you don't have a product team already at your company,
then you can still start offering to do tasks and projects
that will add to your product manager resume,
things like prioritization of initiatives,
technical implementations, user interviews,
and research and that kind of thing.
Secondly, it's very common for people to get jobs
based off of references rather than just applying to them.
This is the case with every type of job,
but so much more so for product management.
But in order to get references,
you need to meet people in the product management industry.
For that, I recommend you go to websites like meetup.com
and look for product management
or product strategy meetups in your area.
You can go to these events.
You can talk to people that are product managers.
You can find out who's hiring and maybe even meet somebody
who would be willing to provide you a reference for a job
or tell you about an opening.
Outside of finding product management jobs
through networking, there's a few places
I recommend you look online.
Number one, go to angellist.com,
which has a big focus on Silicon Valley tech companies
and look for product management jobs.
Number two, go to Hacker News, and they have a,
it's a technology message board, basically,
and you can Google it or you can search on their site,
because they have a monthly thread called,
"Ask HN Who is Hiring?"
Just Google it and you'll find the threads
that I'm talking about.
They do this every month, and it's just full of openings
for product management positions.
The third place I would recommend
is do a search on Facebook for any local Facebook groups
around your city that involve any type of job,
but especially product or technology jobs.
These are really good to browse through
and something good to keep your eye on
in case some listings come up.
I've seen a lot of product jobs go through
these types of groups.
Number four is mailing lists,
and there are some good ones out there.
I recommend a mailing list by a guy named Ken Norton.
You can go to kennorton.com.
It's K-E-N-N-O-R-T-O-N,
and he's a really well-known product manager.
He has a great little newsletter,
but he always has job postings at the end of each email.
All right, so those are some tips on where to look
for your product management job
if you're looking to get one.
And in the next lecture,
I'm gonna give you a little bit more inside advice
on how to go about your product management job hunt.
I'll see you there.

--- Inside Advice on Your PM Job Hunt ---

-: Hey, everyone.
Before we move on to the actual resume,
and interview process,
I wanted to give you a couple
of sort of insider tips
on looking for a product management job.
These are kind of miscellaneous tips
that I just thought that I should tell you about,
and pay attention
because they will help you in the long run.
The first tip is for
any product management job opportunity,
you should make sure that the company,
that is looking to hire has a good understanding
of the product management role.
and can clearly illustrate
why they're hiring one.
I say this because
as an observer of the industry,
I've seen a lot of companies
that don't see the difference
between product and project management.
Sometimes these companies will end up
hiring somebody merely to keep engineers on task,
and if you can,
you want to avoid this
to maximize your experience
for actual product management.
The second tip I have for you is to do
a lot of research on the current product team
or product management leader at the company.
Find them on Twitter.
Look at their blogs.
Check out their job history on LinkedIn,
and see what you think
about the things that they say.
What you're looking for here
is someone that you think you can learn
a lot from that has had
product management experience before.
Sometimes someone will be named
director of product
at small growing companies,
but they'll have no product management experience
before that and this is something that trust me,
you really want to avoid.
On the other hand,
if you're interested in working
for a particular industry or company
down the road,
then working for somebody that has worked
in that industry in the past
is a great thing to know.
It's a great thing to be able to say,
"Oh, I'd like them to be my boss
so I can learn from them.
So pay attention to that."
All right so recap here.
Do extensive research on the people
that you'll be working with,
and use it to your advantage.
Also, make sure that the company
that's hiring you knows why they're hiring
a product manager so that you get a good role
at a good product team where you can learn a lot.
I'll see y'all in the next lecture.
We're gonna move into the actual resume,
and interview process.
Catch you there.
(upbeat instrumental music)

--- Section 14: Review & Recap ---

[No transcript available for this lecture]

--- Resumes ---

-: What's up everybody?
So this section is all about your resume
and interviewing as a product manager.
And this lecture is about your resume
and how we're going to optimize it
to have a better chance at getting a product management job.
So resumes in general, it's true,
not as important as they used to be,
but you still gotta have one, unfortunately,
and you need to make it the best you can
to optimize your chances for getting a job.
Especially in product management
because it's pretty hard to get a job as one.
If you don't have any prior product management experience,
what's important here is to frame the experience you do have
in a way that relates to getting that PM job.
So we're gonna talk about two ways
to optimize your resume here, and they're pretty simple.
All right, step one: If you haven't already,
go back and watch that lecture
on preparing for the PM role with relevant experience
and think through all of the times you've done something
at your current job, even if you're not a product manager,
because it's probably got some stuff in it
that does relate to product management.
Once you've done that, go through and edit the subtext
of each position you've held on your resume
and make it reflect that product management experience
that you've found using the exercise we did in that lecture.
So step two: Make sure that all of the descriptions
in your previous positions, and even the current one,
just make sure your whole resume is like this: quantitative.
All the examples you give on the things you did,
they need to be quantitative.
What I mean by that is we need to say,
instead of describing what your job was,
we need to say, "How did you do at your job?"
This is because as we know,
product management is all about improving metrics.
So what we're gonna try to do is change-
Instead of describing your job, we're gonna change it into
describing how you, in a quantitative way,
improved things at your job.
Here's an example: If you're a marketer,
instead of saying that you managed marketing campaigns,
say how many marketing campaigns you managed,
and how much revenue it generated for the company.
Or do something like "Increased number of
simultaneous campaigns managed at once by 20%
over the course of the year."
You just gotta make it quantitative.
So what we're gonna do now
is we're going to put some fake resumes on the screen.
All right, so this is totally fake. I made these up.
But it's for someone that works in marketing currently
and wants to go into product management.
So you can see the difference
between these two is that we've changed, first of all,
everything to be more quantitative
in terms of what were the things that we accomplished
and then we have also put in more experience
that's relevant to product management role,
rather than talking about stuff that's not relevant
to what product managers do on a day-to-day basis.
So I'll include these in the resources section,
along with some other guides,
on how to improve your resume in general,
but I really wanna just tell you about that.
Product managers all about those metrics
so make sure stuff is quantitative.
And secondly, product managers
do so much stuff that it's pretty easy to think of stuff
that you did that's related to product management,
like prioritization or communicating between departments.
Just make sure that's reflected in your resume
and even if you don't have prior product experience,
it's gonna give you a much better chance
at not being passed over by the recruiter
for an associate or junior product manager position.
All right everyone, I will see ya in the next lecture.
(upbeat music)

--- Interviewing for Product Management ---

-: All right, so you've got your resume optimized,
you've got your online brand perfected,
and you're going to interview with a company.
Congrats on getting that first interview.
In this lecture, we're gonna talk
about some tips to help you get
through the product management interview.
These interviews are tough.
And that's because it's not all cut and dry.
You gotta demonstrate some skills,
usually on a whiteboard, for talking through problems.
So what I'm gonna do
is give you three big tips
on how to nail these interviews.
The first two we're gonna talk about here
and then the most important one
has its own lecture coming up next.
All right, tip number one.
Use every interview to make the next one even better.
Each interview you do is gonna help you a lot
in making the next interview even better.
Whether it's another interview at the same company
or an interview at a different company,
you should pay very close attention
to the questions they ask you
for that product management role.
Then, think about whether or not you are able
to get a good answer to them or not.
For every question, take mental notes
or even physical notes if they let you.
When you go home, start researching those things
immediately.
If you didn't understand something,
you didn't have a good answer, go look it up.
So if you make it to the next round of interviews
for that company, then you have more relevant knowledge
to discuss.
Even if you don't get that job
and you are going to an interview at another company,
this is going to be super helpful
for prepping you for these types of questions
in the future.
You gotta be optimistic here.
These are tough interviews
but each failure is an increased chance
at the next opportunity
so that's how you should look at it.
Tip number two, ask them good questions.
The more research you do before they interview
about the company and the industry and the product,
the better.
You can't do enough, to be honest.
Most interviewers will give you a chance
to ask them questions at the end of the interview
so you should have some really good questions in mind.
Before you go into your first interview,
do that research on those latest initiatives
and industry trends and even what their competitors
are doing, what rounds of funding other people
have raised that are in the same industry.
Use this information to ask them thoughtful questions.
I'll give you an example here.
Let's say I'm interviewing
for a product management position at a messaging app
and they compete with WhatsApp,
which is a really popular messaging app
owned by Facebook.
I'd probably ask them questions
about how they tried to differentiate themselves
for the market and then what they think
the biggest obstacles for them
are in the next year.
If WhatsApp releases a feature in, you know,
a month before you do that interview
or any time in the last six months,
you need to say,
"Hey, what are your thoughts on that latest feature?
"Are you guys gonna try to compete with that or what?"
And this really just shows
that you're interested in their product,
you understand what's going on in this industry.
And I guarantee that if you ask good questions like this,
you're gonna stand out from the rest of the candidates.
All right, let's recap here.
Use every interview as an opportunity
to make the next one better.
Don't cheap out on this, you gotta do it.
And then two, ask insightful questions
at the end of your interview
that are relevant to the product management role,
the industry, and the product.
If you use these tips,
your interviews are gonna be really good
and you're gonna be way ahead of the competition.
You're gonna be even better off
if you watch the next lecture
'cause it's a really good tip.
(jolly music)

--- How to Answer Interview Questions the Right Way ---

-: Welcome back, everybody.
Like I said, this lecture is gonna be one
of the most important tips I can offer you
for interviewing as a product manager.
As you already know, if you've been watching
this course so far and I hope you have,
because what are you doing here if not?
Product management positions are based on making
good decisions based on data.
If someone asks you what you would do for a product
in a particular situation, you never just give them
an answer without mentioning how you came to that conclusion
and then why, and then what are the alternatives?
Here's a real life example from my actual career.
I was once interviewing a candidate for a product management
role at an e-commerce company.
Everyone's like, "Oh, you gotta interview this person.
"She's gonna be, she's great.
"She's got a great resume, great product manager."
So I'm like, "Oh, okay, I'll interview her.
"We'll see."
I asked her if she thought we should develop
a native mobile app, or just continue focusing on just doing
a normal mobile website, like we had been doing.
The problem is without even thinking,
I mean she she had like a three-second delay.
She goes, "Yeah, you should do a mobile app,
"native mobile app."
She didn't give any other details.
So whether or not she was correct with her answer
is not the issue, and in fact it didn't even matter.
The problem is that she just gave an answer
without asking for more information
to help her make the decision, and she didn't
follow it up with any reasoning behind
how she arrived at that conclusion or how her answer
may change based on data.
So you're thinking, "Well, Cole, what was the right answer?"
Look, the answer I was looking for in that situation
was for her to say, "Okay, I need more information
"to help make this decision.
"How many of your customers shop for mobile devices?
"What volume of traffic do you have?
"What sorts of engineering knowledge does your team have?
"And what's your budget?"
These things help make better decisions.
I would've also loved to see her follow-up her answer
by telling me the reasoning behind it.
And then most importantly, tell me what other options
were on the table besides just,
a mobile website or a native mobile app.
Because there are things you can do besides that.
There's an in-between.
You can do a hybrid app, there's other options.
So you need to mention these types of things
when you're answering questions.
As a product manager, there is never
a single correct answer to a question.
There are always multiple possibilities, and it's your job
to synthesize information, to come up with a good approach
and be clear about why this is the best option.
We work on data, not feelings.
Just like if someone in your company asks you
why you're doing a particular initiative,
or adding a certain feature.
You can't just say, "Because."
No, you gotta say, "Because the data says this.
"The user interviews say this.
"The market is going this way,
"and our competitors are doing this.
"but here's the other options we considered.
"This one seems to be the best approach for this reason."
That is how you think and act like a product manager.
So in interviews, always ask clarifying questions
when possible.
It shows that you're being thoughtful and thinking
like a product manager.
By the way, I don't mean to start asking them questions
if they ask you simple things like,
"Where did you go to school?"
Or, "Where did you work before here?"
What are you gonna ask 'em?
"What do you mean by that question?"
No, I mean, if you get to the point where
they're asking you about your opinions on their product
or "What would you do in this situation?"
There are a lot of tech companies out there
Google and Facebook and Twitter
and a lot of the big ones out there,
where the entire product management interview process
is just hypothetical situations.
They're like, "Hey, what do you think about this?
"What if this happened?
"What would you do?"
You got a reason through it.
It's like showing your work on a math test in high school.
Man, I sucked at math in high school.
I don't know why I like this job though.
Anyways, that's a really big tip.
If you can internalize that, and you can start thinking
about these things behind the questions,
and asking for more information to make the correct
or at least most correct decision on the answer
then you're gonna go far.
I'll see you guys in the next lecture for some
real insider tips on actually getting that job.
(upbeat music)

--- Insider Tips for Getting the Job ---

-: What's up, everybody?
All right, fair warning.
This is a longer lecture, but I promise you it is worth it.
I'm gonna let you in on a little bit of a secret here.
This is one of the best ways for getting any job,
but it works especially well for a product management role
and works especially, especially well
for getting your first product management role
in a junior position, which is what you gotta do
if you don't have any experience prior.
We've already talked about how it's very important
to demonstrate knowledge of product management
in order to land the job.
This is why interviews make you demonstrate
this during the interview.
But what about outside of that interview?
Can you do anything to demonstrate
that you really, really know what you're talking about?
Well, if you followed along so far,
you're probably already well underway
to demonstrating knowledge by establishing an online brand
and doing a side project
if you've never had any experience at work before.
But there is one more thing you can do
to maximize your chances at getting the job.
However, I gotta tell you that this is not easy,
this is gonna require some more time and effort from you.
If you aren't serious about getting your dream job
as a product manager and you don't want to go the extra mile
to stand out from the tons of other people
that want that job,
then you don't need to finish this video.
And I am super disappointed in you.
So, go ahead if you're one of those people,
turn off the video.
The rest of us will wait here.
Go ahead.
No? Yeah, go.
Okay, so now that the lazy people are gone,
I'm gonna let the rest of you in on this secret,
but it's not that complex, it's actually pretty simple.
Do a demonstration project for the company
that you're applying to
and send it to the hiring manager and the recruiter.
It's like doing a generic project
that we already talked about,
but it's much more targeted, and that's the key.
You're probably thinking like, "Oh, does this work?
Is this like why am I gonna do this?"
It does work.
In fact, this is how I got
my first few product management jobs
and I have also gotten a ton of offers by doing this
that allowed me to basically choose the company
I wanted to work for instead of having to accept
the first offer that came my way.
So, the basic technique here
is to find out what sorts of things the company
is working on with their product currently
with their strategy and that sort of thing.
Show them how you would approach the problem
if you were to be hired.
You can do this by either doing some generic research
on the company and finding out
through news articles and that sort of thing
what they're trying to build or the challenges they face.
Or you can be even more effective
and ask them some questions
during your first interview with them.
And with all of the work you've done so far in this course
with the online branding and your social media profiles
and just knowing what you're talking about,
it should be pretty easy to get
at least a first round interview or a phone call.
So, here's how I do it,
and you know how I did it
back when I was first starting out.
Let's say that I got a first round interview
for a product management role.
During the first interview, I would answer the questions,
but then I would also ask them why they're hiring,
which features this product position would work on,
and see if they give me any information I could use
on the things that they're trying to build,
or the markets they're trying to get into,
or the clients that they want, that type of thing.
So, what I would do after that,
I would immediately leave, after the interview
I immediately go home and I do research on the product,
the industry, the technology,
and the competition around the stuff that they told me
when I asked them those questions.
Then I would spend at least 24 to 48 hours
developing a comprehensive overview
of how I would approach that problem.
Usually, I would spend an entire weekend doing this,
but, "Cole," you say, "I want to go out with my friends
and drink beer on the weekends,"
or not beer, like I don't know, vodka.
Maybe you should do that instead.
But the other people are going to be doing this extra work
on the weekends in order to put themselves
above the other candidates
and show that they really want that job
and they know what they're talking about.
So, in the past, these projects I've done
have ranged from a small four-page PDF document
all the way to a 60-page slide deck.
I'm not kidding,
I actually put together 60 slides once for a company.
They freaking loved it.
These things usually contain things
like descriptions of the research I did,
the approaches I wouldn't consider,
the technical limitations I've found,
wire frames of potential solutions,
A/B tests I would run, competitive overviews.
and sometimes I even have gone so far
as to find friends of mine that use their product
then I call up those friends and say, "Hey,
can I talk to you for 30 minutes?"
I interview them about their feedback
and their experiences with that product.
I record it, or at least I transcribe it,
and I include those interviews
and the conclusions I drew from those interviews
in that project.
So, once I'm done with the project,
I will usually email the completed document
in like slide deck or PDF
to the recruiter and the hiring manager,
thanking them for the interview,
telling them that I put together some suggestions
to demonstrate my interest in working with them
and what I know about the industry.
So, the silver lining to all of this,
even if it doesn't help you get the job,
is that if you do it
you will get an even better chance at getting the next job.
This is because, first of all,
you've done a lot of product thinking
while you've been putting this thing together.
And trust me, it builds up and it helps,
but mostly because you can just take that case study
and you can just put it on your website or your blog
and then the next recruiter
that talks to you about this position is gonna check it out
and they're gonna say, "Wow
this person really knows their stuff.
Look at all of these case studies they've done
in their free time."
It's gonna be awesome.
On top of that, you can even talk
about these suggestions and case studies
for that company that you didn't get the job for.
You can talk about them
in the next interviews with other companies.
It has so many benefits.
All right, so use this information wisely.
This is really a great technique,
not only for getting a job,
but thinking through that product management mindset.
Like I said, you're gonna have to go through that process
and doing it is just gonna improve your product mind
as you go through it every single time.
Look, if you apply to a company
and you sit back waiting for them to call you
with a job offer
or waiting for them to call you for the next interview,
you're doing the exact same thing as everybody else.
Stand out from the crowd by putting in the effort,
showing them that you really want this position
and demonstrate to them that you've got the skills to do it.
I hope a bunch of you are motivated now with that lecture,
but also the stuff before it.
If you do all of that,
you're gonna be in such a good place
that you're gonna be so far beyond where I was.
I mean, when I first started
I had nearly no experience,
I did not take this course, obviously,
or that would be weird.
At that point,
there were no schools teaching product management.
I had no product management experience,
I kind of had to just figure it out.
But now, you've got someone like me
that's gone through all of this
telling you exactly how to do it.
You are leagues above every single other person
out there trying to get this position.
All right, I hope you enjoyed that lecture.
I know it's a lot of work, but it's totally worth it.
I will see you in the next one.
(upbeat music)
(upbeat music continues)

--- Section 15: Review & Recap ---

[No transcript available for this lecture]

--- The First Things to Do ---

(bright upbeat music)
-: What's up everybody?
So this is all about how to excel at the role
once you've already got the product management job.
And because this is all about
how to excel after you've already got the job,
I'm assuming you've got the job,
and in that case, congratulations.
I am so happy for you.
It brings a tear to my eye.
And if only there were a way that I could
through the screen congratulate you or make you happy.
How about this bear suit?
I will teach the rest of this lecture in this bear suit
with these claws because you make me so happy
to be a product management instructor.
You got the job.
So once you've got your first product management job,
it can be a little bit daunting
trying to figure out what the first thing is
that you should do,
but bear with me here.
Product management is notorious
for having one of the longest ramp up times
for a lot of jobs out there.
By that I mean, it's really usually taking a lot of time
to get completely comfortable with the role.
This is because the work you do
involves so many different teams
and you need to understand the history and goals of each one
to do the best work.
What I'm gonna do here is give you some tips
on things to do as soon as you get the job and start work.
These tips will make you very much more effective
at your role.
All right, so tip number one,
schedule one on ones with everyone in the team.
If you're at a large company,
you don't have to meet with literally everybody,
but be sure that you at least meet
with the leaders of each major team.
Ask them what their goals are and what challenges they have.
Take notes so that you can refer back later.
All right, step two is arrange a meeting
with the lead engineer on your team
and talk about the technical stuff.
Have them explain to you how the architecture looks
on the back end in detail
and what sorts of technologies they use,
like databases and languages and that sort of thing.
Also, ask them what the biggest technical challenges are
and make sure you understand the basics
of how data is stored,
and again, what languages they use
and what the sticking points are,
what they're having trouble with.
This will just help you build a great relationship
with the engineers,
and it will make sure that you know
what you're getting yourself into
if you're gonna try to change something major
on the back end with a feature.
All right, so the next tip is start talking to users.
When you joined a lot of companies,
it's gonna appear as if they know exactly what users want
and even show you user feedback
that they've received in the past
or over the last couple of years.
However, it's extremely important,
or should I say, beary important to talk to your users
and get the best idea of what's important to them.
You wanna see this yourself
because you may draw some different conclusions.
After all, you're a brilliant product thinker.
Some things you can do here
are ask to join in on a sales call, go to sales meetings,
spend some time with the customer service team
helping them answer user questions
and tickets that people send in for support.
Things of this nature is what you're going for.
I also like to do a quick Google search
for things that people say about the company
on the social websites like the Twitter and the Reddit.
Number four, my bear head is falling off,
but read every internal document you can get your hands on.
I recommend, or you ask or find documentation for the epics
and mobile releases that have been made in the past,
as well as things like sales decks
and internal presentations.
All of these things are gonna help you
get a great historical view of the product
and what has happened over time.
Tip number five, look at the data.
As soon as you have the chance,
you should take a look at the existing metrics
for the product.
If there's a data science team or any data analysts,
ask to have a meeting with them
where they describe the types of data that are available
in terms of metrics
and any plans they have around implementing more tracking.
Sometimes you might not think there's enough tracking
to make the best decisions,
and you'll have to make an effort with your team
to track more.
Tip number six, meet with the boss.
You should make it a top priority
to have a meeting with either the head of product
or the CEO to ask them about expectations for your role,
but also to understand their goals.
Make sure what you talk about
is related to what metrics they're trying to optimize for
and what they'd really like for you to do in this role.
What are their biggest problems?
Like what are they fearful of over the next year
in terms of stuff that's gonna be challenging
or maybe what competitors are doing?
You can also ask them about things like
process improvements internally that need to be made
or improving marketing.
These are all things that you can help out with
as a product manager.
All right, with those tips,
you're going to be in a very good position
to do the best work as soon as possible in your new role.
Like my claws are sharp,
and I do mean my claws because I am a bear,
your product management skillsets will be sharp,
your knowledge will be sharp starting this job.
It's super important that you do this.
And I've done this in all of the companies I've worked for.
It's been really huge.
All right, everybody, hope you enjoyed that one.

--- End of Course ---

-: Hey everybody. This is the end,
but only the end of the course, not the end of your journey
into the world of product management.
Evan and I have included a ton
of reference material and content, and as you know
we've got the interviews available to you
which are very good, but we'd really like
to add a lot more content to this course.
If there's any feedback you have, I would love to see it
in the feedback section or just email me
or Evan and say, "Yo, I wanna learn about this"
and we'll make a lecture and we'll put it up there
and we wanna make sure everyone's got
a really good grasp on this, and we're,
we're totally open to clarifying
or redoing some stuff to make it make sense for you.
The last thing I want to say before we get outta here
is that something really fundamental to the role
of product management is that it is not so much
a set of skills that makes you a product manager,
you do have to have these skills, don't get me wrong
and we've already talked about them,
but you really need to have a mindset.
That mindset is all about proving things through data,
making the best decisions based on the information
available, getting more information if you need it,
and just generally thinking about things in a deeper way
than just through your gut feeling.
Remember, as a product manager,
you are almost always wrong and so is everyone else.
That is until you can prove that your assumptions
are not wrong through data and through talking to users.
Lastly, I wanna say that this is the coolest job ever
and I'm so glad, along with Evan, that you guys have
joined us through this journey and learned so much
about what we really, really like doing.
Please feel free to reach out, to contact us, email us,
write us whatever, Tweet at us.
Twitter's kind of cool, Snapchat us, whatevs,
and yeah, just see how you're doing.
If you get a job or if this was helpful for you at all
let us know and we're there to answer questions for you
in the future. All right, everybody, I'll see you later.
Thanks so much for taking this course.
Alright, the course is over.
Go ahead and turn off the video.
Yep. Nope, stop the video.
I gotta go.
I got places to go and things to do, so
Turn it off.
Alright.

--- Interview with Daniel Demetri, Product Lead @ Earnest, Ex-PM @ Google ---

(Jazz music)
(laughing)
(indistinct chatter)
-: Yeah. So I'm here with Daniel Dimitri,
the head of product at Earnest.
And could you tell us a little bit about you,
yourself and your background?
-: Yeah, so I was a computer science major at Harvard.
Studied that undergrad in economics.
Worked in private equity for a year and a half,
and then applied to Google's
associate product manager program.
Got in and started in New York,
moved out here and
worked in ads a bit,
and was, sorry in maps.
Was on the maps team for two years at Google.
And then I left and joined Earnest,
and I've been there for a year and a half.
-: Cool.
-: I was their first product manager
and now I lead a team of eight.
-: Yeah.
Tell us about the APM program
because this is something that not everyone knows about,
the associate product manager program at Google.
-: Yeah.
Well, the Associate Product Manager Program at Google
is a amazing two year program.
They take 30 people a year globally.
Typically, well, it's zero to three years out of college,
but it's typically zero.
They take computer scientists.
They train people up into the nebulous craft
of product management.
And most, I think that they had about
50% retention within five years.
So they do a pretty good job retaining talent
for quite a while.
And they expose you to different parts
of the Google ecosystem.
So you can see ads, you can see search,
you can see Google Plus, back in the day.
-: Do you get a choice on like
where you want to go?
-: Yeah, so I remember my first request was like,
"Please don't put me in ads."
-: Why?
-: And they were like,
"Well, we can't promise anything.
We'll see what happens."
And then I showed up on my first day,
and they have this session
where all the trainees or the recruits of the crop
sort of get picked up by their bosses.
And I get basically my first meeting with my first boss.
And I'm like, "So what do you do?"
I mean, what do we do?"
And he's like,
"Well, I'm the director of the ad exchange."
And I'm like, my face fell.
And I was like, "I'm gonna be in ads, this is the worst."
And they like totally disregarded,
voluntarily disregarded my request to not be on ads.
-: You had one job request.
-: Yeah.
And I actually loved being on ads.
Your second year you have a little bit more influence as to
what you do.
And then throughout the rest of your career,
Google has quite a bit of mobility, so.
-: Yeah.
So before you got into product management and you,
before you were in APM program, like what was
your notion,
your preconceived notion on like what product management was
and what was, why were you interested in doing it?
-: Great question. I had no idea what it was.
My friends...
So I had a friend who was a product manager at Google
and I asked him,
what do you learn as a product manager?
Like what do you become?
And he was basically like, well,
you learn how to get things done.
I'm like, that's super vague, come on.
But he didn't really do a better job at explaining it.
I felt that the role was going to be to marry the business
and the tech sides and being a joint kind of computer
science and economics person, that really appealed to me.
I knew that I liked being engaged with technology,
but I also felt that I didn't want to be coding.
-: Right.
-: And so thinking about technology, but also not coding and,
but being engaged with the coding and the coders.
-: Right.
-: That felt really appealing to me.
-: Yeah.
-: I wasn't really sure what it was.
I just knew that Google's a great place to work.
-: Yeah.
-: And it seemed like a great program.
And so I felt like I'd try it out.
-: So now that you have done it for some time and you're head
of product to Earnest,
if you were to summarize it in some way, like a short way,
which we've actually,
we've had like a whole lecture on this in the course as
well, like summarizing product management.
-: Right.
-: How would you summarize it?
-: Right.
I mean, I think that one better way to think
anything's done might be to fill the gaps
between all of the cross-functional teams.
So everyone in the company is working to make the product a
success in some fashion,
but there tends to be a lot of translation that needs to
occur between engineering and in our case, capital markets.
Because we're lending money out and marketing and PR
and like all these functions need to collaborate,
creating a shared sense of timeline,
creating shared sense of what is being delivered
or when or why,
is sort of core to the product role.
And that sort of manifests in product requirements.
It manifests in roadmaps.
And so I view like,
as a simplified version or defining
what product management is,
I'd say it's something about filling the gaps between the
cross-functional roles.
-: Cool.
We actually actually just got done filming a lecture about
the different types of product management roles
in different companies.
-: Yeah.
-: Actually referring to your blog post,
it's a good blog post.
-: Thank you. Thank you.
I'm excited about it, yeah.
-: Yeah.
Except you have like a different,
probably a more thorough labeling, but
we talk basically about internal product management,
where your stakeholders are internal and then, you know,
business to business and enterprise product managers.
-: Yes.
-: And then just consumer product managers.
-: Yes.
They're very different.
And I think in like resume screening,
I think that like hiring managers
definitely are taking a view as to
oh, that they're that type of product manager.
-: Right.
-: And so one of the great things about Google was that it's
such a broad conglomerate at this point.
You can seamlessly transition between the teams.
-: Right.
-: And allow your functional,
or like your core expertise in computer science or just
academic pedigree generally speaking,
to allow you to transition at a junior level between teams
with actually very different styles.
-: Right.
-: So I worked on Double Click,
very B2B sales driven environment,
which is like features prioritized on
what customers were asking for.
But then I switched to maps,
was entirely different.
It's logs analysis,
What are clients...
I'm stuck in the Earnest.
-: Right.
-: What are users of Google Maps really doing?
-: Right.
-: And analyzing that and then prioritizing and
bug closings and feature requests.
-: Yeah.
-: And so then you can go out from Google or from another
program to sort of address kind of any business need.
-: Yeah.
-: And so that's a really,
that's a great reason to start off a product management
career at a big company.
-: Yeah.
-: Because as you were pointing out earlier in the course,
there's so many different types.
They do very different things to have different values.
-: Right.
-: So getting to experience both is very valuable
for one's career.
-: Yeah.
I've talked to a number of Facebook PMs that apparently
Facebook has a rotation program on,
you're an early product manager.
I think you don't even have a choice but to rotate between
departments or products.
-: Yes.
-: I think like you have to go from like timeline to photos
to whatever and get a good handle
on like all of these things.
-: Right.
-: And it keeps the ideas sort of fresh.
You don't get like a tunnel vision on the,
vision for that one product.
-: Yeah.
-: So that's interesting.
-: Yeah. Google's APM program is not dissimilar.
-: Oh. Really?
-: Yeah.
They, you can convince the leadership
to allow you to stay in a similar area.
-: Right.
-: But they do wanna see you switch your perspective.
-: Right.
-: Yeah.
-: So speaking of features, you said feature requests,
we have a whole section in the course as well on,
so you have ideas and you have feature requests and then,
there's this need to get to the core of a user need
rather than just taking feature requests at face value.
-: Yes.
-: So there's a, I mean,
in the course I talk about a number of times where
I've had a feature request and I do two or three levels
of digging and you find out that there's something,
that there's a,
that that was a symptom,
-: Yes.
-: And that there's more of like a disease somewhere else.
-: Yes.
And I can think of a million instances.
Right?
-: Could you...
Yeah.
Could you tell us about any of these, these instances?
Because this practical real world like storytelling is
really useful.
-: Sure. Yeah.
So I mean right now this week we,
so Earnest is a consumer lender.
We do student loan refinancing and personal loans.
We have an internal team that does some of the loan
processing and they have gotten to the habit reviewing raw
documents that have been provided by the client.
Think like a tax form.
And so they've gotten to the habit of, okay,
I'm gonna look at these tax forms and then I'm going to make
a bunch of calculated
subjective judgments based off of that.
And then I'll using those subjective judgment
put in a decision.
And so they ask for features that are like,
I wanna be able to pull all the documents more quickly.
When in reality if you're thinking
kind of a little bit ahead,
what you wanna do is extract the important information off
those documents and then have the calculations run that
judge the applications quality.
-: Right.
-: And so you don't wanna build features that are going to
make it easier to suck all the applic,
like make it easier to review PDFs faster.
-: Right.
-: You wanna build the extraction mechanism and then automate
kind of a lot of the logic that they're doing.
-: Right.
-: It's a classic problem when you're dealing with an
operations team.
-: Yeah.
-: It's like that balance between automating their job and
making their jobs just more efficient.
-: Yeah.
-: In other circumstances, you know,
you think that you can get away with great examples on
Google Maps.
We tried to get away with a removing a feature called
Search Nearby.
So, Search nearby is a feature where you would search for
Gary Danko restaurant,
maybe you're going on a date there,
and then you wanna find a bar next door for afterward
to meet up with friends.
So you would click search for Gary Danko and then click
search nearby and then search for bars.
And we thought, well, can't people just type
in bars near Gary Danko?
-: Right.
-: Or just search for Gary Danko
and then the map will move
and then they can search for bars.
-: Right.
-: But then,
and the opposite to people requesting features is
the opposite would be removing features people
aren't worried about.
-: Right.
-: And fixing what ain't broken kind of can lead to.
We removed this feature search nearby and got,
this became our biggest chunk
of customer feedback at Google.
And the maps team by-
-: By removing that?
-: By removing that.
-: Yeah.
-: The consumer operations team is the team at Google.
That sort aggregates customer feedback.
Our user feedback and-
-: Oh, there's a whole team.
-: There's a whole team.
-: Wow.
-: That clusters the user feedback.
I think very few people outside of Google are even aware
that this happens.
-: Yeah.
-: Because Google has a habit of not really responding
-: Yeah.
-: To user feedback.
In some circumstances they do,
but actually the user feedback that's clustered by a team in
India and then the product managers reviewed the clusters.
-: Right.
-: It's the only way to digest this information on.
-: Sure.
There's still probably just so much, so much information.
-: Exactly. So the top cluster quickly became,
where does search nearby go?
-: Right.
-: And we're like oh shoot.
-: That's awesome.
-: To redo this.
-: Yeah.
This is...
I met a guy that was a PM of bugs
at Facebook.
So there's a person just-
-: Wow.
-: That they have like one person,
or I guess many people in charge of bugs,
but it's the same thing.
They just have such a volume at like one and a half billion
that they, you just can't look at bug reports.
-: Right.
-: Like you have to do some analysis on the bug reports.
-: Right.
-: Because there's hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of
thousands of them coming in.
Right?
-: Yes.
-: There's no way you could ever read them.
-: Which is another classic trade off product managers have
to make is between closing bugs and working on new features.
Especially when those features are going to render obsolete.
Some of the bugs have been been filed.
-: Yeah.
-: And you're-
-: More features for that matter.
Yeah. Feature.
-: That's right.
Or we could add that check box,
but eventually the whole page is gonna go away.
-: Right.
-: So what-
-: I think you're ignoring them.
-: Yes, it's the worst when people,
I mean most product managers are probably hated in some
respect in their companies because they're the
no people or the like not yet people or not now.
-: Always.
-: That's not a priority.
-: Right.
That's the classic.
-: Yeah.
-: We'll prioritize it.
-: Yeah.
Well yeah, that's P four, P three.
Well we'll get, that's a next quarter thing.
-: Right. Right.
-: I think that that's what...
If you generalize the view of
those sorts of decisions,
what you realize is that product management
is a very path dependent exercise.
-: Yeah.
-: Because you might agree on the vision
with a bunch of people,
which is probably the first goal of product management is
just to agree where we head of product.
Like with Spotify,
it's like what do we want people to do with this product?
Is it, do we,
is the vision more like adding videos in this is a
multimedia center or is this more just like a music?
This is your like lay time.
-: Right.
-: This is the background of your whole life.
-: Right.
-: Like, which is not video.
-: Right.
-: Background of your life is probably more like just silent
and responsive and aware of you.
-: Yeah.
-: So then, okay,
we agree on the vision of where this is headed.
Where of course the vision at Google
will just be all things to everybody.
And then-
-: Yeah.
-: Which made it very challenging in fact
to direct priorities.
-: Right.
-: But then, okay, how,
what's the roadmap to getting there?
What are the coins along the playing field that we've,
we're gonna navigate off course to pick up.
-: Right. Right.
-: And so it's like a video game.
-: Right?
-: Like should we pick up the 2 cent piece over there even
though it's kind of further,
because if we do actually it's close,
it's a traveling salesman problem.
-: Yeah. Yeah.
-: Which is why we're not really worried about product
management being obsoleted by technology.
-: Right.
-: Cause we're having to crack that one.
-: Right. Exactly.
Computers can't crack the traveling salesman thing.
-: Exactly.
-: Yeah.
-: And that's so we're safe.
-: Yeah, exactly.
Actually the first thing you mentioned the off
the feature ideas where you have to dig deeper with
the documents being pulled at a faster rate.
That is like the classic example of the,
I think the Steve Jobs quote where if you ask people what
they want, they'll just say like a faster horse.
-: Yeah. I've heard it attributed to Henry Ford even.
-: Oh, okay.
No, it's, it was-
-: Oh, but Steve was probably the one who made it popular.
-: Yeah. Right.
Like everything. Yeah.
Didn't do it first.
-: Certainly no, like,
I think very few people were asking for the iPhone.
I remember I was pretty excited to have a separate iPod from
my phone and that they just seemed like
different functions of my life.
-: Then only later iPhone V3,
gen three was really where kind of came together as this is
gonna offer some pretty compelling new experiences.
-: Yeah
-: And simplicity for one's life.
-: Right.
-: Yeah.
-: There's...
Yeah.
As you know, Apple is,
I guess they're notorious for not doing, like,
I think they do user research but not doing
the broader-
-: That's right.
-: High volume.
-: The vision doesn't come from the bottom sum.
It is top down vision
and then they'll validate the execution,
but they're not deriving strategic insight as much-
-: From people
-: Exactly.
-: From the average consumer.
-: Yeah.
-: Because back in,
I think you can still look at some pictures like in the
early or late 1800's and their version of the future
was like horses and buggies on the moon.
-: Right.
-: You know, so somehow we're gonna get the horses
and buggies up there.
-: Yeah.
-: But the...
But it's still gonna be a horse and a buggy.
-: Yeah.
It's not dissimilar, companies like Google,
Google takes a very supply side driven approach,
which I think makes them differentiated in the market.
They're really focused on what can we do
with all this technology.
So given that we have big table,
like how can we apply big table?
-: Right.
-: Given we have search, how can we apply search?
And-
-: Big Table is their database of everything.
-: That's right.
-: That's the single table.
-: No, that would be, they have the knowledge graph.
-: Okay.
-: That's the database of everything.
Big table is the database, storage technology.
-: Right. Okay.
-: But, okay, so if you do have the knowledge graph
and you do know a bunch of things about things-
-: Right.
-: Then where can we...
And then, so like I integrated that into Google Maps
and other product managers were integrating that into search
as like knowledge panels.
-: Yeah.
-: Other people are integrating that into Android.
So you're just geographically located somewhere.
It's pulling up relevant facts
about your life in Google now.
-: Right.
-: And so these sort core technologies and data assets,
the product management team then translates those into cool
features and ultimately products.
There was a whole product Google had called,
I don't remember.
And as you navigated through a foreign city,
it would sort of craft a story for you to follow along.
It was like guided tours,
I think it was called like Sidekick or something like this.
-: Yeah, yeah.
One of these Google side things that just comes out.
-: There's a million of them.
-: There's a lot, yeah.
-: Yeah.
-: Sometimes they die quietly.
-: Right.
Sometimes they don't get publicized in the first place.
-: Right.
And so at companies like Google and what we're trying to
create at Earnest,
it's awareness of what technology you have that then
translates into products and features,
which is reasonably different from how product
management is approached at other companies that, you know,
might focus more on user research,
-: Right.
-: Or focus more on customer demands.
-: Right.
-: And it's just thinking, what can we do with this?
-: Right.
-: Yeah.
-: Okay.
So if I am not mistaken, you actually have,
you have job listings out
for product management for Earnest?
I mean, it probably will not be the case
when this course is out, but-
-: No we're gonna be constantly hiring folks.
-: Okay. Okay.
-: Don't worry.
-: But-
-: We've had it open for a year and a half a year.
-: Oh really?
-: Yeah.
It's just you're constantly hiring.
It's how the team goes from just me to eight people.
-: Right. Okay. Yeah.
-: By that thing being open.
-: Okay.
But you, so you're still a person
I'd like to ask about,
because this course is about getting into product management
for a number of people, how would you, first of all,
suggest someone goes about learning about the role,
obviously this course,
but ultimately looking to get a job there.
-: Yeah.
-: Techniques of and such for finding them and that
sort of thing.
But then also,
what do you like to see as a-
-: Yes.
Hiring manager.
-: As a hiring manager.
Like what qualities are you looking for?
-: For sure.
So product management,
learning about it is tremendously difficult.
It's very hard to craft a product management internship.
We do do it at Earnest and we are hiring interns.
-: Yeah.
-: Including interns with no product management experience.
-: Oh, that's good then.
-: Yeah. So that's relevant.
-: Product management interns-
-: See what Tiffany wants.
-: Great.
Product management internships are probably a
good exposure.
I didn't do one. Right?
I didn't know about in product management internships when I
was in college and afterward it I didn't need it.
So, but I've heard great things about it
from my friends who've done it.
Okay.
Otherwise, to learn about product management are
to work in a product environment.
So I've definitely had a number of engineers
sort of approach me
and they're interested in product management.
They don't yet have enough experience working
with product engineering.
-: Right.
-: So they might be very infrastructural-
-: Backend.
-: Engineering.
That sort of thing.
-: And so I encourage them to transition closer to products
prior to jumping onto the product management team so that
they can observe and participate in the product decision
making process before they're, you know,
in a role that is exclusively focused on that.
-: Right.
-: So front end engineering or just product engineering
generally speaking,
is a great way to get closer to product management.
Once one has a sense for what product management is,
the hiring managers are looking for folks, you know,
at Earnest we're looking for four things.
So we're looking for analytical skills, technical skills,
creative skills, and collaborative skills.
So that's like at Earnest, I think other companies,
based on the type of product manager they're looking for,
they might switch it up.
But I can speak to Earnest really well.
And so analytical skills,
we're looking for things like proficiency and SQL.
Do you know your different join types?
Do you know how limit works?
-: Sort of a basic proficiency, but not to-
-: To an extent to...
Up to the point of an advanced proficiency.
-: Okay.
-: Would be even better.
-: So yeah.
SQL is a database technology.
And then you would learn MySQL, which is,
or you'd learn SQL queries,
which is being able to query the database for like-
-: Exactly.
-: Information.
-: Any kind of...
In disparate data sets, you know,
Excel junkies can kind of,
are most apt to be able to transfer into, SQL experts.
-: That's not too different.
-: Exactly.
You're just dealing with understanding data
at the highest level, right?
Maybe you're a R or a state junkie, like wherever data is,
if you're capable of thinking through basic statistics or
thinking about how you can tie data together
to derive insights,
you're on the path to making data driven product decisions,
which are the types of decisions that you'll be able to use
to get the respect of your team.
-: Yeah, that's a big one.
The respect of your team
-: Yes.
-: Sort of thing.
-: Yes.
-: Is being at least, even if you're not technical,
you should, there's a product manager
named Ellen Chisa, who actually went to,
is called Boston near you and I,
and she has a really great article about this.
Like if you're not technical, like
how technical should you become?
And she argues that it should, at minimum

about learning more, right?
-: Yes. Yes.
-: Doesn't mean you have to go be an expert coder
this weekend,

single new technology
-: Yes.
-: That you're using and always asking, you know,
engineers about things and yeah.
Demonstrating interest and in learning these things rather
than just saying, I don't care about these things,
we'll have an engineer deal with it or whatever.
Or I'll ask an engineer every single time.
-: Yeah, Yeah.
Which is gonna be an expensive conversation to keep having.
Right?
-: They're very expensive people.
-: Yeah. Yeah.
So that describes kind of the second bucket of skills that
we look for as technical skills.
-: Yeah.
-: So we're looking for folks who have done engineering
or have done computer science class or you know,
have learned programming through a general assembly thing,
and then applied that in some respect on side projects.
-: Right.
-: So ultimately your ability to understand how the technical
architecture has implications for product extensibility is
what is core to the technical knowledge, as well as not
getting shown up on the technical side
by folks like your designers.
-: Yeah.
-: So like that's just kind of impairs trust
in a manner of speaking.
-: Right.
-: So I challenge all of my product managers to not get shown
up by our product design team.
-: Yeah.
-: Which is, you know, relatively advanced design team.
They're getting into get,
they're making some copy changes, style changes,
-: Right.
They're publishing those,
-: Right.
They're dealing with unit tests
failing and understanding the build failed.
What does that even mean? The build failed.
-: Right.
-: If you don't know what it means for the build to fail,
you still need to ramp up your technical skills before you
can really get into a technical product management role.
-: Right.
-: Maybe you'd be product manager of like
a toothpaste company,
but of a tech company, you need to understand the,
what's the process of building the right system.
-: Right.
And even if you're not pushing code yourself, you know,
to production, you need to understand how the flow works.
-: Right.
-: In continuous integration and like what get is and what
branches are and what.
-: You need the vocabulary.
-: Totally.
On the creative side, I usually challenge people
to imagine what I mean by that.
-: Yeah.
-: And then on the collaboration skills,
-: That's a good one.
(laughing)
-: On the collaboration skills, that's like,
I think of communication skills,
I think of getting things done
without stepping on too many feet.
Communication skills I've also further broken down
for folks, it's the ability to speak with accuracy.
So you, what you were saying is true,
which is actually sometimes challenging,
especially when you don't know something.
It can be easy to speak about truth with things that are
actually inferences or you're uncertain about them.
So speaking with truth is accuracy,
speaking with precision or communicating with precision.
So that would mean saying things in an unambiguous manner,
they can't be misinterpreted.
-: Right.
-: And then speaking with brevity,
so even if you were to speak with accuracy and precision,
you might still have that coded
in tons of other information.
And really great product managers are able to communicate
with all the certain teams in those three respects.
-: Yeah.
-: Briefly, accurately, precisely.
-: Right.
-: And then additionally they're able to translate,
they use, we use words that sort of communicate
with each of the teams.
-: Right.
-: Like legal is going to have a different perception
on what a policy is than engineering.
-: Yes.
-: Because engineering has a,
like a rounding policy and a data persistence policy.
-: Right.
-: Legal doesn't really care about data persistence policy.
They care more about the products,
implications for users
and how are users being charged things.
-: Yeah.
The communication thing is huge.
You need...
There's a saying which I don't know if it applies anymore,
but for every single group of stakeholders,
so you executives,
your legal and marketing and engineers and design,
there's a different deck, right?
So there's different-
-: Yeah.
-: Different words for different groups.
-: Yeah.
-: Like you say,
they should all still be concise and brief
and true to the point factual.
But then if you're talking to an executive, you,
it's gonna be ultra brief, right?
-: Ultra, yeah.
Elevator at Google, you were known to have to,
especially in the earlier days, to chase around executives.
-: Right.
-: And so that you could catch them at an elevator moment.
-: Yeah.
And then give them your 30 second pitch
and get their verbal approval and
you'd run off and keep executing.
-: Right.
-: That was how brief you had to be
to win as a product manager back in like 2007.
-: That's awesome.
-: Yeah.
And people would stock,
there'd be like a chat group where you're like, oh,
Marissa's in the elevator, like grab her.
Yeah.
-: Yeah.
-: Fortunately we're not like that yet at Earnest.
-: So then, so you mentioned the four things,
and let's say that someone has,
they've learned about what product management is and they
have learned some technical skills and so on and so forth,
and they've applied some technical skills
and made to decide project and that sort of thing.
-: Yeah.
-: And they're looking not even for a job with you,
but just anywhere,
is it vital to have some sort of portfolio thing
project to point at or?
-: Yeah.
That's a great question.
I think that what I've been looking for has been a little
bit less portfolio based
and a little bit more experience based.
We wanna see that people have gone through the trials of
interacting with a really top class product designer and
knows kind of how to communicate.
As we were just talking about,
one of the core competencies is being able to translate with
the different functions.
That's sort of the ultimate purpose of the role.
-: Right.
-: And so if you have created like an independent portfolio
that you've product managed this product on your own,
it doesn't demonstrate that you have the skills to
communicate well with the product design team and understand
what their concerns are gonna be or their considerations or
what you can delegate to them.
Similarly with a great product engineer,
similarly with a great, you know,
maybe even a legal team or marketing team.
-: Right.
-: And so we're looking a little bit less for
independent work, and a little bit more for
experience working with these teams.
-: Right.
So a product internship role and-
-: Yeah.
Or even like, you know, in coursework,
like a cross-functionally collaborative product, sort of.
-: There could be a side of product as long as,
you're actually doing this communication between the
different groups and you're-
-: Yeah, exactly.
-: Right.
-: It just can't be like a tiny group.
-: Right.
-: Like of one you and one other person.
-: Right.
-: You're wearing too many hats.
-: Right.
-: In that role and you're not like practicing
the scaling of those hats being worn by different people,
then you coordinating all of them.
-: Right.
-: So, okay, for entry level product managers,
we're really just looking for basic proficiency in those
skill areas more so than experience.
So we're looking for, you know,
do you know computer science fundamentals already
and do you know SQL
and do you, you know, have a track record
in the creative side of being able to problem solve
interview questions with us in the whiteboard.
-: Right.
-: Or of academic thought,
like do you have a cool thesis?
On the collaboration side you know,
did you participate in any group projects extracurricularly?
So you know,
at Harvard there was the like robotics competition,
you could like jump into that and help build a better robot.
Or did you just show leadership by becoming president
of the school newspaper?
-: Right.
-: These are sort of like the core collaboration skills or
indications of those core collaboration skills that will
give us confidence to bring you in as a product manager at
the entry level.
But beyond the entry level,
going to mid-level and senior level,

with the types of stakeholders that you're gonna be engaging
with at our environment.
