--- Interview with David Lifson, VP of Product @ Homepolish ---

(upbeat music)
-: Hey, everyone, I'm with Dave Lifson out of New York,
VP of Product for Homepolish.
And we're gonna do a little bit
of interview here on some product management topics.
And Dave has been...
Dave, you were at what?
You were at Etsy before like long ago
and what, Amazon as well?
-: Yeah, so my first job out of school was at Amazon.
I was a software developer
and then realized within probably 15 months
that I was made to be a product manager.
And so, it got to switch
to the recommendations team at Amazon,
which is because you bought these items,
we recommend these others.
And I was at Amazon for a total of two years,
learned a ton, loved it there,
but just had an opportunity I couldn't say no to,
to move to Brooklyn from Seattle
and be the first head of product at Etsy.
So, that was 2008 when Etsy was 50 people
and all three founders were there.
And I then did my own startup for three years
with the technical founders of Etsy.
We sold that company, I joined General Assembly
as employee 25
and grew that from 400 when I left,
over two and a half years.
I started running curriculum,
and then moved to running product design and engineering.
I spent a year at Poppin doing the same thing,
product design and engineering.
And then, now, I just started at Homepolish
about a month ago with the same.
-: Cool.
So, yeah, this course has about,
right now it's over 4,000 students
that are enrolled, and it's only been out for about a month.
And it's focused on, yeah,
people that are new to product management
or people that are in the role
and they're trying to get better.
And yeah, one of the recurring themes
as I know that you know from teaching at General Assembly
and that sort of thing is that product management,
learning about it is quite popular right now,
because it's becoming a more popular role.
People are starting
to learn what product managers are doing,
but they want to find out more,
but there's no good way to learn about it in a formal way,
or you can't get a degree.
You have that.
You and I both just happened upon it.
And I think, most people end up doing that.
And I think, right now, due to things like General Assembly
or like this course,
you can learn about it formally
from an actual product manager.
So, the first question I wanted to ask was, (chuckles)
it's a interesting one, no matter who I ask is like,
if you had to sum up the role of product management
in a sentence or two, what would it be?
What does the product management mean to you?
-: Oh, wow. Yeah, that's really great.
So, what does product management mean to me?
I think, the role of the product manager
is to have a deep understanding of the customer,
a deep understanding of the business,
and marrying those two together
to identify opportunities and problems,
prioritize those opportunities,
and then devise the right solution for the right time.
And so, what's so challenging
I think about product management is that
we don't fit the mold of the individual contributor.
I think, organizations, historically,
have been structured similar to
how they were in the industrial revolution.
With manufacturing where you just,
you have departments that have workers,
and workers create output.
And coders write code and marketers create marketing
and designers create designs and what (audio breaking)
Don't create anything (audio fast-forwards)
for everyone else that we work with in the organization,
so that the quality that is produced is higher
and the solution is more efficient and more effective.
-: Cool.
Okay.
And so, what did you think about the role
before you were actually a product manager?
Did you have any preconceived notions
or did you know anything about it?
-: Well, my first team at Amazon,
I was in the community group,
which did user-generated content.
And so, my little corner of it was this thing
called Listmania where
Amazon customers could create lists
of their favorite products,
like your top 10 things to take with you on a camping trip
or my favorite scary movies.
And we had no product manager.
It was just a bunch of engineers.
And we just built whatever the heck we wanted to build.
And so, I ended up...
Jeff Bezos had this idea.
This was back in 2006,
where Facebook was becoming a thing.
People were talking a lot about networks.
And so, he was like,
"What if we created a network of product SKUs?
If products could have relationships to each other,
what would that look like?"
And somehow that ended up on my desk as a 23-year-old.
And so, we decided to build this hot or not for products.
So, essentially, customers could vote
on which of these two shoes is more stylish
or which of these two electronics (audio breaking)
easier to use.
And as you've voted (audio breaking)
we would use your votes
to generate you a recommendation (audio breaking)
like very expensive.
We built the whole thing.
We launched it and it was a total disaster.
Conversion rate was somewhere around half a percent.
And it was such a clear example
of how product management could have saved the day,
and we just didn't know it.
-: Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
Working on the right thing rather than just something.
-: Yeah, and or testing any assumptions
or doing any sort of user research or prototyping.
And so, when I moved into the recommendations team,
which did have
a track record of having strong product managers,
I was surrounded by people
who could really show me the ropes,
which is how I learned.
-: Cool.
So, something that we talked, 'cause you mentioned,
you mentioned this like talking to users
or validating something and validating your assumptions.
Another thing that happens quite often
to us as product managers is we talk to users
or we get feedback and they send some sort of request
or maybe if you're an internal product manager,
you get basically what amounts
to a feature request or product spec request.
And oftentimes, that thing that's being requested
is not what is actually the solution.
It happens to everyone.
If you look two levels deeper,
there's actually another problem here
that maybe that solution doesn't solve.
And so, when you're receiving feedback from users,
you gotta dig in deeper.
Do you have any war stories
of user requests that are just absolutely
were just not the solution at first that you could share
and maybe what the real solution was?
-: Well, I don't know if I can tell you the real solution,
'cause I don't know,
but I'll tell you a story of this exact thing,
except it wasn't (audio breaking) it was a founder.
So, the end of 2012 and the founders of-
-: Hey, sorry about that.
So, we had slight technical difficulty. (laughs)
-: It's okay. Do you want to ask the question again?
-: No, no, we can just totally edit it in.
So, you said it was the end of 2012
and that's-
-: Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah,
okay, all right,
(Dave drowns out interviewer)
so let's keep going.
Yeah, so it was the end
-: Yeah.
-: of 2012 and the founders were really worried
that the business model
was looking a lot like University of Phoenix
in the sense that our revenue came from
adults coming to a classroom and learning.
And so, revenue was really constrained
by one, our ability to purchase or rent real estate.
And then, secondly,
our sales ability to fill that real estate.
And so, they came to me and they said,
we really need something that is a subscription
and can target customers
who don't live where we have classrooms.
And you have three months, go build it.
And so, this is like, this was a big mistake for me,
because as a product manager,
I should have recognized immediately
that there was no mention of the customer here
or any sort of customer problem.
This was something that came out of a spreadsheet
and in an analysis of market valuations
and what it would be really nice if this cell
in our Excel spreadsheet was twice as big.
And in some cases, it is up to the product manager
to translate that into, okay,
well then, I'm gonna go do some music research
and discover what a customer problem is,
and then work towards product market fit.
But given the three-month timeline on top of that,
we felt really pressured to just look around
to see what assets we had available
and what we could package together.
And so, we did happen to have a library
of live streams that we had recorded and stored away.
And so, we said, okay, how about for 25 bucks a month,
you can have access to our library of videos?
And we called that front row.
And so, we built it, we launched it.
It did okay,
but no matter how much we optimized the signup flow,
the UX, optimized the acquisition marketing channels,
the LTV just really wasn't there.
And I still today, feel like at its core,
subscription is just
a very odd business model for education.
So, yeah, that was always a tricky one.
-: Yeah, yeah.
I've got, yeah, tons of stories myself,
but that's the one thing all PMs have in common.
So, there's always these weird,
yeah, user requests and yeah,
I think, in your early years,
you probably just go do those things
and then you realize that's a big part of your job
is taking all of those
and figuring out what the real underlying need here is.
And in another interview we did
that we were talking about that quote that I think,
the Henry Ford quote which is like,
people would've asked for faster horses
instead of the car back in the day,
'cause they just can't see past a...
Not everyone's sitting back and thinking big all the time.
And Apple takes advantage of this with their products
by not asking users what they want,
but rather, seeing what people want through,
yeah, usability testing and all of that.
And then, giving them stuff
that they didn't know they wanted or needed.
-: Yeah, this is really the hardest thing
I think for a product manager,
because when you're starting out in your career,
I think, you wanna get some traction,
get some projects under your belt,
just practice the process.
And so, it's much easier
to take things after product market fit.
You're in optimization mode.
How can we take our signup flow
and increase conversion by 10%?
And in that case, you do your user testing,
you suss out what those
steps in your funnel that are giving you the most trouble
and then you just tweak them.
And so, it's very like, here's my problem,
here's my solution, and solve it.
But when it comes to inventing the iPhone,
it's such a big open-ended problem
that it requires I think, a very different approach,
where you really wanna take more of that ideal style,
user-centered design approach
of let's just like a very open mind,
just talk to people
and learn their context and their situation,
and then take these leaps of faith
and do a whole bunch of prototyping around it.
Yeah, it's very difficult.
(interviewer laughing)
-: Okay, so just a couple more questions.
One,
because yeah, not only are you a teacher.
You've taught product management in the past,
but you also are a hiring manager, now VP,
and before that, head of product,
if you were to
look from the inside out
and say if you're doing it all over again
and you didn't fall into the role like you or I did,
but you were actively trying to get into the role.
Let's say in modern, current day,
how would you recommend that someone with very little
or no experience tries to work their way
towards getting a product role?
What skills maybe should they learn
or how should they look about approaching it?
-: Mm-hmm. Yeah.
So, (clears throat) I think,
yeah, so I think, the way to do this is to really show
that you understand the product management process
and that you're able to apply that process
to a lot of different situations.
So, the process in my mind is (clears throat)
identifying what you think the problem is,
what you think the customer is,
what you think the solution is,
going through this iterative approach
to getting to product market fit,
being able to draw upon a lot of different tools in order to
validate those assumptions.
All those different MVPs that you wanna use.
And I think, that there's a lot of different ways
in which you can demonstrate your understanding of that.
You can go to a hackathon
and work with people and just,
in the span of two days, apply the process.
Or perhaps you work in marketing or you work as an analyst,
you can still get involved in the development process
at least perhaps in the user research part
or the opportunity analysis part.
And so, and that way,
you can then actually structure your resume,
so that it highlights
all of those different steps of the process.
So, as a hiring manager, I can look at it and be like,
okay, they don't have the product manager title,
but they completely get what the five steps are
and they've shown that they've done those steps in the past.
-: Mm-hmm.
Yeah, okay. That's really good.
So, this is some of the advice that I give
and that in terms of yet prepping for the role
is first of all, look internally
is one of the easiest ways obviously,
but if that you're not able to do that
and you're trying to apply somewhere,
then you should look at things you've done
in the past that have to do with product management.
And since it's such a vague and wide-ranging role,
most everyone has done something
that a product manager will do at some point,
like cross-team collaboration
or project management
or coming up with a few different ideas
and finding the best one with data.
Even people in community operations,
customer service, marketing,
people do some things in these roles
that are product management style things.
And then, shape your resume to explain those things.
That's what I'm hearing from you.
-: Yeah, let's say you're an account manager,
you talk to customers all day,
so put together what you think
the product roadmap should look like
and then support
your proposal.
What is the data behind your prioritization?
What is the user research behind your prioritization?
What are the risks and assumptions
that you would wanna validate
for each of the ideas in your roadmap,
so that you would feel confident
that executing those roadmap ideas
would in fact achieve the desired goals.
-: Yeah, absolutely. Okay.
Okay, so just for the last question,
if someone is, let's say, very familiar with the stuff
and they're looking at just,
they're just looking at learning real hard skills.
So, either technology or UX or something like that.
Let's say they're going to take a PM course somewhere
and then they were also gonna go learn a skill.
What skills would you recommend
that people be either really familiar with a technology,
or something that really stands out to me
as being a big benefit on someone's resume.
-: Well, certainly, they're gonna need to understand
just basic agile project management principles.
And so, being able to break things down to user stories
and sprint planning and sprint retros,
and just the mechanics of that.
I think, wire framing is something
that they should be able to do.
They don't need to learn a tool like Sketch or OmniGraffle.
I think, it's fine if you just grab a marker and paper
and just can draw,
but you need to be able to communicate
what you see in your head to other people through pictures.
And then, on the technology side,
I think, people have the most anxiety
about the technology piece,
and what I would tell you is spend some time
understanding how the internet works.
So, if you type a URL
into your browser and click go, what happens?
And you should be able to go really, really deep
on what happens, 10, 15 different steps
between when you hit go
and when the page shows up on your site or on your browser.
And I would not
feel like you need to learn a programming language.
I don't think you need to learn HTML and CSS.
I don't think you need to learn Ruby on Rails.
As much as it is more important,
just learn internet architecture
and just how do web servers work.
-: Yeah, and things like
in the course I talk a lot about
you just need to understand what okay front end is
versus back end and where things are being calculated,
like where the logic is living on the server
or the front end,
or how do things get to your browser or your device.
-: Yeah, and I think, if you want extra credit,
you could probably spend some time learning SQL,
because a lot of times, product managers need data,
and so sometimes they have to go get it themselves,
because there's no data department to do it for them.
-: Right. Awesome.
Okay, so I think, we are just about out time,
'cause we're in different time zones.
You gotta get to work.
But thanks a ton for this.
And yeah, do you have any, I don't know,
websites or Twitter handles or anything,
blogs that you want to pitch as a last thing
for the students that wanna follow you on there?
-: Follow me? Oh.

--- Q&A Section 2 (part 1): Introduction to Product Management ---

(lively music)
-: All right, so we're back.
We're gonna do some Q and A.
So there are some questions throughout the last section that
we're gonna talk about and discuss.
Hopefully this will help you guys get a little bit more into
the content, be a little more enriching and whatnot.
So section two, actually,
I'm gonna be asking questions and then we have a couple of
questions from students that have submitted them,
and we'll ask them and then discuss them.
And generally the way these are gonna work is that I'm gonna
be asking the questions because I'm not a product manager,
so I'm just gonna be learning alongside and making sure that
there's nothing we skipped or you guys think is confusing.
All right, cool.
What do you think, Cole?
-: Sounds good.
-: Sweet.
All right guys.
So section two, we talked about,
what do we talk about, pretty much being a product manager,
What are products,
we cover differences in product management,
project versus product.
We talked about the day in the life of a product manager.
So I just wrote down some questions that are, I think,
relevant to that,
relevant to people especially who are interested in becoming
a product manager, getting the job specifically.
Hopefully these are some of the questions
you guys were asking.
These are things that just kind of peaked my interest
as we were going through.
And I'll kind of start from the top.
Okay, so in the first lecture we talked about product,
you know, what is a product manager?
And he, you know, talked about the ambiguity of it,
how you're not a manager,
how you kind of sit in between
so many different circles and whatnot.
And the next lecture we talked about
what exactly is a product.
So one of the questions I had was,
if you're running a service business,
do you have product managers?
How would that work?
You know, cause there's a lot of big companies,
big startups that at the end of the day are,
are actually services.
They might be technology enabled services,
but do you just not have product managers in that case?
(lively music)
-: Yeah, it actually depends on, of course, the business,
like everything else, but I guess
I'll take your question to mean like agency style services,
which is, hey, we need a website or an app,
how much is it gonna cost?
And they give you a quote
and then they do that thing for you.
This is how like marketing agencies
and advertising agencies work,
PR agencies, that sort of stuff.
So is that right?
Is that what you're wanting to know?
Like are there product managers in those situations?
-: Yeah, pretty much.
I mean, an ad agency is a good example, right,
of like a service business.
You're not, whatever they make,
they push off to the customer
and it's made by individual people
and essentially they're hiring people
when you hire an agency.
So, yeah.
-: Yeah.
-: What does the role
look like there?
-: So there is,
historically, there's not a product manager
because I mean, historically,
I mean, you know, in the past there's,
there hasn't been much in terms of product management there,
but there have been project managers
in those businesses because basically when a client says,
Okay, we need to do this,
what happens is they pitch the thing that they would do.
So ad agencies will pitch, okay,
well we're gonna make this website for you,
we're gonna make these ads.
Here's some concepts like you've ever seen
that TV show Mad Men where they,
they basically pitch clients and the clients are like,
We love this.
It's better than the other, you know,
people that are pitching us, so we're gonna hire you.
In that case, everything's pretty much decided beforehand.
So you don't have the scope changing it,
like everything's decided.
So it's a thing for a project manager.
You basically have a budget
and you have a date perfect for project management.
But there is now a trend towards
having product managers at some agencies.
And that's because agencies are now starting
to do a little bit more full service sort of thing.
So they'll say, okay,
we'll make you an app,
but we're not gonna just make it and hand it over.
We're gonna make it and maintain it for you.
And so they now are hiring product managers at some agencies
or some service businesses to basically almost, yeah,
work on it, work as a full-fledged product manager
on an initiative for a company.
-: That sounds incredibly awkward for that product manager.
Like talk about stakeholders because you'd have to manage
the product for an external client that has their own
decision making hierarchy.
-: Well, it's sometimes the agreement is okay,
we're experts in,
if I were the company that was doing the service would say,
look, we're experts in iterating
and looking at metrics and all that.
So these are things that if you're hiring an agency to do,
you're probably not good at,
or maybe you don't know anything
about like app development or whatever.
So they will usually say, like,
the product manager at one of these agencies would say,
hey, okay, we already delivered you the app,
but we're gonna make some changes and make a V2 of it
because we see in the metrics or the way that, you know,
culture is shaping up lately that we can improve it by doing
this and this or we're gonna do it.
And it's usually not like an approval thing,
like they have to ask the client every single time.
It's more like an implicit like agreement beforehand that
okay, for like a one year period, we're gonna main,
we're gonna build this first and we're gonna maintain it
and improve it consistently.
And of course we'll provide you with reports
on how we're doing it,
but there's not usually like a decision sort of hierarchy
that has to be gone through every single time.
-: Okay, cool.
All right, so moral the story students
is that Don Draper is not a product manager
if anybody watches Mad Men.

Let's see, okay, and so in the lecture,
what is a product?
This is a question that I was kind of asking myself,
or at least I didn't have clarity on this.
So we were saying that product can be anything, right?
It could be a button, it could be a feature,
it could be an entire page.
And that the term of product is very liberal, right?
And it's very kind of amorphous and it's really more or less
decided based on the importance of whatever is in your
broader product or broader platform.
I was just curious because you guys often work on teams,
so I was curious, how does that break down?
Do you have, you know, you might, let's say you have
a product team on,
you can use that Twitter example of a product team that
focuses on the sharing options of a tweet, right?
But then do you have,
does it go up to a higher team that's in charge of the,
you know, Twitter tweets in general than the higher team?
That's the Twitter timeline and then the higher team,
which is like, you know, the best product managers.
Like how exactly does the hierarchy in groups work?
(lively music)
-: So yeah, like every other question, it depends,
but I can give you some actual like,
real life examples and tell you about a couple arrangements.
Okay, so, first of all,
there's some companies that structure,
usually smaller companies that structure product management
around platforms, so there will be a team
and a product manager in charge of like Android and iOS,
or just Android,
another one in charge of iOS
and then another one in charge of the website.
And that's usually becomes pretty inefficient as you get
much larger because you have teams that are not usually in
sync and your Android app might end up looking different
from your iOS app because the teams are not the same teams
working on the two different features across those two apps.
So what you end up getting is these things called
feature teams, which we've talked about in the course.
And that's, you know, like you're saying, it's a,
okay, I am a product manager
in charge of let's say Facebook's newsfeed.
I'm also in charge of it on, you know,
iOS on web and on Android as well.
And those teams usually have engineers and designers
that own all of the, you know,
the design around the newsfeed on all platforms
and then also the backend systems
that run those things on all platforms.
So there is like, what you're getting at here is like,
how are those teams like in terms of hierarchy of teams,
Is there one?
And yeah, a lot of times there is,
so you will have, as another Facebook example,
you will have like someone in charge of all of the newsfeed
in general, right?
This is probably like a director of product,
director of product newsfeed or something.
And then you have product managers, not directors,
but just normal product managers and product
and their feature teams
that are working underneath that person.
So one product manager for let's say video in the feed
and another one for the ranking algorithm
with their team and then another one for advertisements
in the newsfeed and so forth.
I don't know if those are the exact teams
that they would have,
but like they do sometimes have these hierarchies together
and it depends on the company,
but they do group 'em up and usually
those groups are overseen
by what's called either like a product lead or a
group lead group product manager or a director.
And then, you know,
then even directors get managed by VPs at really large
companies by like Google for instance.
They have so many different products that like they have
chief product officer and,
and then Senior Vice Presidents and then Vice Presidents
and then directors, and then group leads and anyways,
but yeah, they do have hierarchy.
It just depends how big the company is
and you know who the company is.
-: Yeah.
How do you guys, let's say the,
in the case of you have a product manager who is in charge
of, we'll go back to Facebook, you said on the newsfeed,
who's in charge of the algorithm
for what displays in your newsfeed, right?
Like something really pretty granular and he just owns that
space and then you have a product manager who is in charge
of the broader newsfeed.
Does it happen a lot that you guys have disagreements?
Because one person,
I mean ownership in that case
is very kind of tenuous, right?
Like one person might, since he's the algorithm guy,
he might have very strong opinion about the algorithm,
whereas the person above him, you know,
is trying to take a broader picture approach.
How do you guys reconcile differences like that?
-: Yeah, that is this,
this kind of goes into something that I go into
with the career section of the course,
which is when you become a product manager somewhere,
if you ever have a boss that is flat out
telling you what to do as a product manager,
then you're not a product manager.
There are some cases when you are, you know,
a junior or associate product manager and the decision
making power of like, what should we do next?
You know, with all of these expensive resources
of the company, the engineers and designers
is a very serious decision.
And so you as a product manager
are told what to build or what to accomplish,
but you can kind of approach it in the best way possible.
But in in general,
that's about as like prescriptive as it gets
as a product manager.
So if you are a product manager, you,
it is up to you and your team of what to do.
Otherwise it's against the nature of the position.
And so if you do have these sorts of disagreements,
then there can be disagreements, that's fine,
but there should never be a directors
for instance that says, no, don't do that thing, right?
The best directors or the best managers or group leads,
are going to not ever say no to you as a product manager
on what you guys wanna do with your team.
But they'll ask you if they know
that it'S the wrong thing to do,
they'll ask you questions that will lead you in the
direction of knowing that, okay,
maybe this is not the right thing to do.
And then the other part of that,
the other side of that is that,
as we've talked about in the course,
there are goals that are set up for the company.
So there should be very few disagreements because you know,
if your company has a goal that's like, okay,
you want to increase user retention by 5% and then you want
to increase new users by 10%,
then it's kind of a narrow window of stuff to do in there.
Whereas if you don't have any goals at all,
like you could do anything.
So there's a lot of room for disagreement
on what you should do.
But if it's like, hey,
increase retention by 5% for everyone in product this year
or everyone that's working on the product,
those are our two main goals,
then there's less room for disagreement,
if that makes sense.
-: Yeah,
I mean I guess it'd just be at that point disagreements
about how to get to that goal, right?
-: Right, but you as a PM again,
your whole job result revolves around
proving it through data, right?
So you're never guessing
and if you're guessing you're not doing your job,
but you want to prove it and have indisputable proof,
quantitative proof and qualitative research proof
because you are again,
the expert on the user and the expert on the product
and you've done the research, that's your whole job.
-: Got it.
Yeah, okay.
So the next question I had was,
when you're describing kind of the dynamic
between teams, right?
So you'd say that sometimes
you have a product manager on one individual feature page
or product,
but then also sometimes you have teams.
I'm kind of curious,
how do you guys delineate work within
the product manager teams,
so let's say you have five people on one thing.
Like what are you guys doing on an individual day?
You all doing the same thing and fluidly coordinating,
or is it more the case that within the group of five
you guys have set, you know, distinct roles?
(lively music)
-: Well, the term product team
is not a team of product managers.
It's like a product manager
and designers and developers and you know,
depending on the company,
maybe a data scientist or something like this.
But then you,
so that's also called a feature team sometimes.
And that makes sense because it's usually a team working on
a specific feature.
But, you do have,
if you use the the term like a normal English product team,
a team of product people,
it goes back to that first question, which is,
which is it's usually led by a group,
group lead or a director.
And yeah,
they do usually have narrow slices of features
to work on or user behaviors to work on improving.
So there's very little like stepping on toes
I guess you might say.
But there does need to be like communication
between the teams because
one team might change something in one area
that changes something in another area.
And so they need to,
you know, say, okay, this is what we're doing, this is why,
here's all the research,
talk to the other product team and the other product manager
and say, you know,
we wanna get your approval on this before we do this thing
because it's gonna affect your future.
And that happens all the time.
It's a big part of product management is just communicating
and approving and this sort of thing.
-: Okay, cool.
Yeah, kind of sounds like a taking a little,
literally I for some reason thought that it was like a group
of product managers all hanging out in one group.
So you're saying it's more of a,
it's a product team, right.
Okay, so let's see,
when we talked about what kind of PM you wanna be,
it seems like you kept alluding over and over
to the fact that some of them
are in general easier than the others.
And specifically you said that B to B or
it appears that you kind of are implying that B to B PMs
have a easier job.
Is that something you generally would say,
and what metric are you kind of
using to evaluate easiness versus hard?
(lively music)
-: So I wouldn't actually say that one is easier than another
because they all have their difficulties, right?
They all have their like pain points.
It's, but I do mention
that like consumer product managers usually
do really well under like uncertain pressure,
where things are not always certain because you don't
actually know if you're doing your job right,
you're building stuff that's like innovative and,
and you know, take Snapchat for instance.
They are building stuff that like literally has not been
seen before in terms of social media applications,
and media sharing and and connection between people.
So they're trying to like invent new
this new paradigm and keep iterating on it,
keep making it the coolest and the best thing.
And that's a lot of pressure because they're having to be
really creative and test a bunch of new stuff that,
and it's stuff that even users out there don't really know
that they want until they try it.
And that's the kind of pressure you get
uncertain sort of creative pressure,
but it doesn't mean that it's necessarily harder,
or easier than like a B to B product manager.
Because while you have less uncertainty
with business to business because you know,
a business will tell you exactly what features they want you
to build, you know,
in order to renew their contract next year,
you have a lot of other types of pressure like from
sales deadlines and you know,
navigating complex hierarchies, you know,
the corporate world of the people you're selling to
and making sure that you understand
that the differences between
the customer and the user,
like we've talked about
or like we talk about in the course quite a bit.
So, and even internal product management
is also difficult because you have
a different type of pressure on top of that,
which is,
although it's more certain what you need to build because
your coworkers are there,
or your marketing team or customer service or whoever needs
the thing you're building is saying exactly
what they need, what kind of functionality they need.
You have other sorts of pressures,
like you have limitations on the types of technology
that you have implemented at your company
and like you have limitations on the budget
for the maybe a new tool you need
to purchase in order to get the data
from one place to another.
And then you have the,
you have to navigate the approval hierarchy internally
at your own company to change these processes.
So it's, they've all got their difficulties.
They're not any much,
some of them are not harder than others,
but for beginner product managers,
I think the one with like the least distractions
are gonna be the internal product manager
and the business to business role
because it's just a little more cut and dry.
But the consumer role is probably the most common one.
-: Well kind of what I picked up was that it's,
there's two dimensions that make one of the,
or make them more tricky or less tricky.
And that was one the size of your user base, right?
So internally your user base might be 10 people, right?
Or then if you're at B to B
it could be hundreds of people
and then if you're at B to C it could be millions.
And then the second element was your ability
to talk to them.
Like internally you walk down the hall, B to B, you know,
you can schedule a meeting with them and then B to C,
you know, you can only talk to so many,
the feedback loop with your customers can only be so tight
and it's easier to establish a feedback loop internally
or with a customer that's on contract with you already.
Is that a fair enough assessment?
-: Yeah, so that is true.
Business to consumer is gonna have
the highest potential number of users
and you can't possibly talk to them all,
of course depends how successful your product is,
how many people are using it.
But, yeah, there's companies like Salesforce
that are business to business
and they have potentially millions of users out there,
but they don't have millions of clients.
Actually, I don't know how many clients
they have in terms of companies that are paying them money.
I would imagine it's hundreds and hundreds of thousands,
maybe in the low millions, but it's not, you know,
a hundred million 'cause there's not a hundred million
companies out there just to buy it, right?
But there's a hundred million Twitter users,
at least in fact over 300 at this point.
But you get when you,
when you're doing like a, yeah B to B for instance,
at a volume like Salesforce,
you still get, when someone is paying you money,
a lot of money usually for software, they're a little more,
you're in contact with them,
you got their email addresses,
you got all of all of the stuff,
you can reach out and schedule meetings with, you know,
people that are frustrated or people that usually just,
they're happy to say, yeah,
I'll tell you what I like or what I don't like.
But, it's not quite as easy
when you are a consumer product manager
because users are not super interested
in talking to you unless
they're super passionate about the the service
or like product you have.
They're just kind of using it like, you know,
how how many people do you think out there
are like using Twitter
and then also really, really, really wanting to go,
you know, do a user test for Twitter.
Like some people just use it and they use it.
They're not super crazy about it.
-: Yeah, I was gonna say I almost think that the,
in B to C, the more passionate they are,
it almost degrades the quality of their response, right?
Like in Twitter,
the angriest people and the most passionate people
on Twitter are the worst people to talk to.
They're just the typo machines that don't make any sense.
They probably support Donald Trump for president
and they're just, it's like,
I always think at Twitter like a hundred,
it's like a hundred million kindergartners
and you have to round them all up.
So I would imagine that's a problem.
-: That's actually, there is a thing that happens with,
you know, user bases and in B to C
and that is the more passionate someone is about
giving you feedback.
You're kind of doing a filter there
like a filter bubble and like,
hey, there's the people that actually email you
or that actually like give you that feedback willingly
without asking are people
that have a certain usage behavior,
they usually are really, really big users of your product.
And so sometimes if you're trying to make your product more
usable for a super casual person
yeah, then you are,
you don't wanna talk to the super engaged users as much
because you're not building features for them
'cause they clearly already understand it.
Actually, that's funny because Twitter
has actually that problem right now.

but I know that like they need to move

for like your more casual users

about what they think.

or they don't have internet.
And so you have to think of the super casual one
who's learning how to use the computer

And actually a little like fun bit of knowledge about
these users that, you know, Facebook is now going,
like you said, to the countries
that are much more underdeveloped,

and they even have an app called Facebook Light,
which is designed for phones
that are underpowered so to speak.
But they also,
the interesting nugget is that it is more common
in these other countries to have a phone number
as an identifier and not an email address.
So you think that like asking someone for an email address,
you know, as a login is a pretty reasonable thing.

are developing countries,

No one uses 'em,
what they use is SMS through text messages
and like your sim card is basically your identity.

--- Q&A Section 2 (part 2): Introduction to Product Management ---

-: Let's see. All right
let's move on a little bit.
Let's talk about your job a little bit more.
One question I had was
how much of your job do you think is listening?
And then how much do you think is actual work
like working on some-
I know you could say listening is work
but I'm gonna draw the line between, you know, listening
sitting there talking to people about problems or hearing
other departments and actually wire framing or actually
working on specs or something like that.
I mean, it reminds me kind of
of venture capitalists often refer to themselves
as professional listeners.
And so I was kind of curious
how do you think that works as a PM?
-: There's actually kind of a joke, I guess
in the PM community that we're like
professional email archivers.
That is kind of true
and I even mention it in the course
which is, you spend a lot of your time in email
and just other forms of information. Looking at slide decks
looking at metrics, looking at
if you have like a interoffice chat
like Slack or something.
You're just absorbing information
or listening, like you say, a ton.
And that's a huge, huge part of the job.
But even more important than that
is then you take in all this information
you kind of store it in your head and you synthesize it
based on the other information that you have from whatever
other teams and then you see, okay
well what does that mean for the stuff that we're doing?
And then you communicate it back out. Like I said
you're the communications hub and the enabler.
So sales says something, marketing says something
the executives and strategy people say something
then another product team says something.
Then you get some new metrics from another team that did a
study on something. You read and hear all of this.
You think about what does it mean for our team
and our product and our goals.
And then you basically turn around and communicate that back
out to your team on
okay, here's what this means for our team.
But then you communicate it back out to those other teams
and say, ah okay, so now
we're gonna angle ourselves in this direction to kind of
connect with the goals
that you guys are working on right now.
So there's tons of this back and forth.
I would say it's a huge, huge, huge part of the job.
I would say this communicating and listening thing is
it's like 60% of your time.
It's not all, you know, doing fun wire frames and
that sort of thing. It's a lot of analysis
but it's actually, I find it pretty fun.
You get to talk to new people all the time.
The day's never the same and lots of different mediums of
information that you get to look at.
-: Yeah. Okay.
So 60%
so those of you guys who hate listening, don't become a PM.
You also probably have bigger problems in your life if you
hate listening, but one of them is, don't become a PM
Hey, one question I had.
So when we were doing day in the life of a PM
you said that you are constantly hopping along all of your
social signals, as in reading Facebook comments
reading people who mention you on Twitter
looking at your app store reviews and things like that.
How often are you kind of stepping on the toes of
your communications people? 'Cause you know
traditionally in business structures, you have directors of
communication and that's one of the things they do is they
kind of regulate the conversation between customers and the
reviews and the company, you know, and that's usually like
what are your interns gonna do while they're not
Snapchatting? Isn't that what traditionally interns do?
They just read your Facebook posts
and delete people who say racist things?
-: So there, yeah, that's a good question because
okay look, if you're at a really small company
there's usually
usually you're the one getting product feedback.
So you'll have like a product feedback email address
or something, and it just goes straight to you
if you're one of the only product managers or the only
product manager, or if you're the CEO or you're a founder
product feedback is coming straight to you
but also the customer service email
like the technical support or depending on the company
you know, payment issues or whatever, all of that stuff
you're also getting that. But as you grow as a company
you do get like these teams, these communities
operations teams or customer service teams.
And the tricky thing is, here's a piece of advice
if you are looking to be a product manager
or are one already
you never want to completely remove yourself
from user feedback.
So, as an example, at the company I work at, SoundCloud
we do have tons and thousands of people that email us.
And you know, some of it is product feedback, some of it is
hey, can you help me reset my password sort of thing.
And community, the community team does give us broad
feedback based on, okay
well there was this many requests for this feature
and this many for this other feature.
But if you're not careful
you can just
you can make it too macro.
You don't want to be in a position as a product manager
where you're not yourself
literally yourself with your team and your designer talking
to the users. So no matter how comfortable it gets
even at Facebook
they get literally tens and tens and tens of thousands
of feedback, you know, feature requests
or bug reports every day.
And there's no way product managers can sit there
and look at all of that.
So they have systems that aggregate all of that.
Or like we see with later in the course
with the interview with Daniel
he worked at Google and they had artificial intelligence or
machine learning systems that would basically like bubble up
the top pieces of feedback
for the product managers to look at
but they were not able to look at the actual
sentences from those users or actually talk to those users.
So in that case, even still product managers
and their teams really need to make an effort to bring users
into the office or go out into the, you know
streets basically, get out of the building
and talk to users.
Just one-on-one, face-to-face
and the way I do some of that
obviously we do that as well at SoundCloud.
We have user testing and talk to users
do interviews with them and that sort of thing.
Like you talk about later in the course, Evan.
But we also, myself, like I mentioned
I also make an effort to just go on Twitter
and tweet at people and say, hey
thanks for the feedback.
What do you think about this?
Or why would you use that feature? And this sort of thing.
So you always wanna mix.
So you never want to remove yourself
from the actual feedback itself.
-: Okay.
Let's see.
Oh
this is a question.
Are you required to fast during work and do you just work
with like the skinniest people?
For you guys who didn't notice this
during the day in the life of a PM
he didn't mention lunch, which I thought was interesting.
Do you just never eat?
-: Yeah, when I was making my notes for that lecture
I totally forgot about lunch, but I do.
I do eat lunch and yeah, it's usually quite good.
But yeah, I eat lunch
but if you're a real good product manager
you'll never eat, ever.
In fact, you'll never even leave the office.
-: Yeah. That's cool
-: So that's something to aspire to, I guess.
-: (chuckles) I usually just assume that you eat lunch
at a reclaimed wood standing desk
with your left hand, you know, has a fork
and your right hand has your Twitter account.
You just never stop working.
-: That's not too far from the truth.
In fact, sometimes I just sit there
and watch feedback come in from the app store
or Twitter while I'm eating on my screen.
You know, why not?
-: Wow. Yeah, that's dedication.
Let's see.
Well I had a question about sprint plannings.
Yeah.
So you talked about running sprints with your team
and I was kind of curious
what exactly is your role during sprint planning
and how exactly do you influence the group?
Because traditionally in a sprint, you know
you have developers, designers, a QA person
and often a project manager
or someone who's in charge of the business requirements
and they all have individual things that they work on.
And they all have the technical expertise to do that.
And typically during a sprint plan
you're planning out what stories
if you're doing XP
or what
what is it, I don't even remember what Scrum calls them
what exactly you're gonna be doing during the sprint.
And part of that requires that you know, for instance
adding this feature is a big deal, right?
And so we should put this later or knowing that there are
dependencies between the tasks and things like that.
So how do you get around the fact that you can't influence
people on those technical decisions, yet you know
you are definitely part of the sprint planning process.
-: Yeah.
So for those of you that aren't familiar with sprints
and sprint planning and all of that
we're gonna talk about that later in the course.
We have a whole section on, you know, actually Agile
and Scrum and what sprints are
and all of that sort of thing.
-: Think it's next section actually, yeah.
-: Yeah, okay so
basically sprints are, yeah, the time
it's a time box, usually like two weeks
where you divide your work into
as a team, you say okay
in this next two weeks we're gonna do these things.
And sprint planning is the meeting that you'll hold
basically every two weeks in order to plan and basically
kick off that sprint.
Sometimes they're called sprint kickoffs or whatever.
We call them sprint planning.
And what you're usually doing in there is
well, sometimes you can combine them with another type of
thing called a retrospective
which you'll learn about also in that section, but
there's not a set rule book for these.
And so I can just tell you how I've always approached them
and how my teams like to do them.
And that is basically like we go in and we talk about how
things are going, you know, how the last sprint went
which is part of the retrospective process.
And then we talk about, then usually after we all talk
about that together usually the engineers will bring up
some maybe difficulties
or challenges they're facing on the technical side or maybe
everything's going really smoothly and they don't.
At that point, you as a product manager
and with your designer, your UX person
You're usually trying to basically set expectations for
what's gonna come up in this next sprint
but also the sprint after that
and even the sprint after that because you have to, again
you have to scope and estimate things
that you're not working on yet.
And usually you can do that sort of thing at sprint planning
so let's say we're doing a project.
We'll call it project A. That's gonna take four weeks to do
which is two sprints.
Well, in the middle of those four weeks
we might start talking about
okay, while we're going through project A
we might actually start talking about project B
which is the next one coming up
the two weeks after that
or even four weeks out.
And the purpose of that is that you basically bring data
and then you show the engineers
and the rest of your team and the engineering manager even
and say, hey, here's these users we've interviewed
and they've talked about this problem.
Here's the metrics and the data
and here's the customer feedback.
And you know, the designer and myself have
made up six different potential solutions
and we've tested them and this is what we're looking at.
What do you guys think?
Is this feasible from the architectural side?
So it's all of this.
This is where a lot of the team cohesion comes in
and you never want to, I talk about this later
in the career section actually
you never wanna treat engineers like an agency.
You don't wanna just say, here's what we're doing
go do this. You want to get their input
because a lot of times they'll be able to tell you, hey
well if we just do this one thing or use this other database
over here, we could actually do this.
If that would be easier. And then we would say, oh my God.
Yeah, that might be great and we'll go test that instead.
So you wanna bring them in on the process as early as
possible and just ask them what they think because a lot of
them actually are really into the product as well.
And then besides that
you're just doing a lot of estimation and scoping for stuff
that's pretty far out. So yeah
the stuff you're working on now
the stuff you just got done working on
but then also sort of
setting the place basically for what's coming up.
-: Cool. Okay.
I had one quick question before we get to student questions.
You can, I hope, hopefully this is a quick answer.
Do you suggest people learn SQL?
-: Yes, I do suggest it.
It's not absolutely mandatory.
Depending on the company you work at
you potentially won't have to do any SQL queries.
We'll talk about this later in the course by the way
what SQL is.
SQL is a query language, basically, to query a SQL database.
And the most common SQL databases is like my SQL or MySQL.
And so this query language, SQL
it's just a way of typing out stuff in order to get data
from a database so you can
query what are called tables in a database and understand
how you can take a database of people
of users over here in a database of
user IDs and then couple that with the amount of money
they've paid maybe or products they've purchased.
And you put all that together and do analysis on it.
But I do recommend you learn it.
It's just not absolutely the first thing you should do
if you're getting right into product management.
But if you do learn it
or if you're already an engineer
or a technical marketer and you know it
it is gonna be a huge benefit for you.
And it takes a lot of load off people like data scientists
and engineers when you can go get the data yourself
from the database and
-: Not bug your developers all the time. Yeah.
-: Exactly. Super useful, will do you tons of favors.
I would say if you're gonna be good at one thing
technically as a product manager
SQL and just data analysis is gonna be awesome.
-: Okay. Cause I mean
the reason why I ask is I just think
it's on top of mind for a lot of our students because
Udemy pushes SQL courses really strongly.
It's like the least technical, technical language out there.
It's probably the easiest to learn
and you can learn it pretty quickly.
So it was a question that I saw
that a lot of people have been asking.

Hey guys at home, so if you guys wanna send us questions
feel free to.

you can do, if you want us to actually feature the question
send us a video of you asking a question.

point it at your face and ask us a question
and we will put it up here and address it.
So if you guys wanna do that
just private message one of us
and we'll set it up.
All right, so we got a question
from one of our students here.
-: Hey guys, I'm Jack and I've got a question for you.
From a product management standpoint
what do you think about Twitter?
I like the activity. I never realized just
how much junk there actually is on Twitter
that possibly no one ever uses, you know
all these people contacting you and they just want to
sell things that are a bit sordid that you don't wanna see.
What do you think is going on there?
Maybe the team doesn't agree on anything, you know?
I don't know, but
I'd love to hear your thoughts on that, please.
-: All right, cool.
So we got a Twitter hater. What's up?
-: All right, so Twitter is an interesting company.
I talked about 'em a little bit earlier
but for those of you who don't know where they've started
they were, I believe they were even
they were a different company first
and they pivoted into Twitter.
-: It was Odeo. Yeah, they were a podcast listing service.
-: Yeah. So then actually they became a
like the very first iteration of Twitter itself was like a
text message update thing where you could basically text
message a number and it would set your Twitter status
right? And so there was no web interface
it was just over text message.
-: Was it just for keeping groups of friends updated
with what you're doing?
-: Yeah, exactly.
It was that sort of thing and it then expanded
pretty rapidly, you know, from there.
But, they've come a long way.
But not to be too lengthy with this answer
'cause it could go on forever, obviously but
they have a very useful tool that has turned into
basically one of the most instantaneous sources
of information on the planet basically
and very useful for all sorts of stuff from business to
politics and activism and this sort of thing.
Really, really cool.
But the thing that they have been struggling with
and you might have seen the news in the last couple years
where their user numbers are plateauing and people
aren't using it as much. And that is asymptomatic
of them saturating this market that I already talked about
which is like the really active users
and they need to become more accessible for casual users.
And so they're doing a couple things.
Well first of all
they had their CEO removed, Dick Costello
and then Jack came back.
Jack, one of the original founders, came back.
-: And he came back with the beard.
The beard is back.
What?
Did you see that?
When he came back he just has like a lumberjack beard.
But anyway, what you were saying.
-: Yeah, so Jack came back
and trying to instill some product change and
kind of bringing it back to its roots and make it
more accessible for casual users.
And so they've implemented these features like moments
and they've implemented better direct messaging
and character limits are removed in direct messaging
and they've added, you know, photos and GIFs
and direct messaging and the most recent
controversial change is the ranking of the Twitter timeline
and they're doing it slowly
but this is the same thing that Facebook did and they did it
quite early and they've done a good job of it 'cause they've
done it for so long.
But I don't know if you remember
if you've been on Facebook this long
but there was a time when people were really scared
of Facebook doing ranking to the newsfeed.
In fact, there were people angry about the newsfeed
when it first came out too.
So you're always gonna have your core users that are upset
about changes, but if you do it correctly
you handle it correctly, then they end up saying
oh, well it's not so bad.
Instagram is actually doing the same thing now.
I mean it's getting so popular and so hard to use for casual
people that once you start following tons of people
it's like almost impossible to keep up with it.
So they're trying to show you the best content first
and Twitter's starting to do this and we'll see how it goes.
But overall, I think they have a storied history
a little bit of difficulty
some rocky points there with the executives
and the product management
and now they're kind of trying to get it back in shape.
-: Yeah.
I mean I didn't even know that about the ranking on Twitter.
I don't use Twitter very often at all.
Do they rank it visually?
Like you can see that there's some marker that says it's the
top or you just mean organically
like the top ones just float to the top?
-: It's, yeah, there's no visual.
It's just the same as Facebook so, right.
You know, and it's not even fully implemented.
They're testing it in a lot of areas.
In fact you had to opt in to it at first
but instead of just saying literally the top of your screen
is the last person that tweeted that you follow, now it's
gonna be okay, if I'm gone for a week and I log into Twitter
seven days later
I'm gonna have to scroll back a long way to see who tweeted
something five days ago if I wasn't online.
And that's the kind of friction that casual users are
not really into.
They don't wanna have to be on it every single day.
So what they're doing is saying
oh well, when you log in in seven days
we'll show you this one tweet that was really great
that you missed from five days ago.
We know you're interested in it because you've clicked on
this person's stuff a lot
and you've talked to them through direct message.
And so they rank all of these things
just like Facebook does.
They'll show that to you first so you can feel like you've
not missed out even though you haven't been logging in
you know, every hour for every day.
-: Yeah. So you think that's a good product decision?
-: Yeah, it's an interesting one.
They could do some other things as well but
they have to go that path.
But, the thing here is that you can't
anger the people who want a chronological feed
or instant access to a pure chronological stream of stuff
because some people, you know
news agencies or whoever, they want to get the very latest
and they don't want algorithms like sort of messing that up.
So you always have to be careful with your users in this
sense and either give them that in some other way or
or just, you know
enable them to turn it off or something.
So, you do gotta be careful.
-: Yeah. That's interesting, 'cause it does kind of
grate against the ethos of Twitter
or at least how they came up. Right?
'Cause a lot of people think of it as
democratization.
Any little person, even if you have a hundred followers
has the chance to tweet something that someone can see.
Right? That's why it's so good for social movement.
Tons and tons of people at the bottom saying something
will get attention.
Yeah. So that's interesting.
